turns.content: I'm trying to enforce a hard memory limit on my Rust binary in the nix environment, but I can't seem to get it working. Here is what I have: let ulimit = libc::RLIMIT_AS; std::fs::write("/proc/sys/fs/nodma", "1").unwrap(); unsafe { libc::setrlimit(ulimit, &mut limit) }; println!("Limit set");

The code you posted attempts to write a file and then call setrlimit directly on the raw libc constant. In Rust it is better to use the crate nix::fcntl to wrap these calls safely rather than using unsafe blocks with raw pointers. You should define an nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit struct that holds the limit value, then pass that to nix::fcntl::setrlimit instead of writing to /proc directly.
I tried using nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit but it says I need to implement a trait or use a different struct. Let me look at the actual type definition in the crate and see if that matches what I'm doing.

The Rlimit type in nix is actually nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit which wraps a libc::rlimit structure. You do not need to implement any trait for it because the crate already provides conversion methods from Rust structs to the c types required by setrlimit. Just create an instance of that specific type and pass it along with the resource identifier like RLIMIT_AS or RLIMIT_FDATA.
Wait, I thought I had to manually map the values and convert them myself because the documentation didn't show an easy way. That sounds too complicated for a simple limit setting.

The crate handles all that mapping internally so you don't need to do any manual conversion or custom traits. You simply pass the nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit instance containing your desired limit and the appropriate resource identifier like RLIMIT_AS directly into setrlimit without any extra steps.
I'm getting an error now saying that I cannot construct that type correctly. It seems like the fields are not what I expected or maybe there's a missing dependency in my Cargo.toml.

The nix crate must be added to your dependencies with exact version constraints before you can use those types without compilation errors. Ensure that nix = "0.27" or higher is specified in your Cargo.toml file so the Rlimit struct matches the current API.
That fixed it, but now I want to verify that this actually prevents memory growth at runtime like setrlimit should. Can you explain how nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit interacts with the kernel once set?

Once you call setrlimit with an nix::sys::ucred::Rlimit instance, the kernel enforces the soft and hard limits immediately for that process. Any attempt to allocate more than the specified soft limit will fail with SIGKILL or SIGSEGV depending on the exact configuration, which is why this approach is effective for enforcing caps.

I've been running a Java app with the JVM's ulimits, but I want to try this in Rust. I got my basic rlimit setup working using std::fs::Permissions and maybe some libc calls, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to enforce these limits specifically for memory or file descriptors.

You're on the right track with libc, but the idiomatic Rust approach uses the nix crate which wraps those low-level syscalls cleanly. The key is using nix::fcntl::setrlimit to apply your ulimits before your process starts or via a signal handler if you need dynamic enforcement. This avoids mixing unsafe blocks where you don't need them.
Okay, so I should use nix::fcntl for the actual calls? It feels safer than raw libc pointers. But what about the data structures—how do I represent these limits in Rust?

Great question. The rlimit struct from the nix crate has fields like rl_rlim_t for the limit value and rl_rlim_cur for current usage, plus an is_valid flag to check if your settings are actually accepted by the kernel. This makes it much easier to reason about resource caps without manually parsing sysconf constants.
That helps a lot with clarity. But I'm still worried about performance—setting these limits every time feels heavy compared to Java's ulimits which just read the config file at startup.

You're right that it adds some overhead, but in Rust you can use lazy initialization with std::sync::OnceLock so the first call is expensive but subsequent ones are nearly free. Also, since these limits apply to the whole process, setting them once at startup saves repeated syscalls compared to Java's per-thread checks.
I see. But what if I need to adjust memory caps dynamically? Like during garbage collection or under load?

For dynamic adjustments you can use nix::signal::sigaction to register a handler that calls setrlimit when specific events occur, though this is complex. For most cases, setting limits at startup with the appropriate ulimits is sufficient and avoids the signal handling complexity entirely.
What about file descriptor limits? I want to cap how many open files my Rust app can have without hitting the OS default.

For that you use nix::fcntl::setrlimit with RLIMIT_NOFILE to set your maximum fd count. This prevents your Rust program from accidentally opening too many files and hitting system limits, which is especially useful when dealing with I/O heavy workloads.
I've read about rlimit in nix docs but I still don't get how to actually enforce memory or file descriptor caps in my Rust program without hitting errors.

That's a common stumbling block for beginners. Think of resource limits like speed bumps on a highway: they exist to stop cars (your processes) from speeding up and crashing the whole system, not just to restrict you personally. In nix and Linux systems, setrlimit lets you programmatically define these speed bumps before your process even starts moving. Rust's std::fs::set_rlimit trait wraps this capability so you can safely configure memory or FD caps in your code without needing to write raw syscalls.
So I need to call some function on a limit object? And what exactly am I setting there like soft limits vs hard limits?

You're using the rlimit struct which holds both soft and hard limits for different resource types. When you set these values, the kernel enforces them at runtime so your application can't exceed those boundaries even if it tries to allocate more memory or open too many files. The soft limit is what your program sees and uses directly while the hard limit acts as a ceiling that only root (or elevated privileges) can raise.
But I keep getting permission denied errors when trying to set these limits in my production environment.

That usually means you're running without sufficient privileges or trying to modify hard limits. For most applications, focus on setting soft limits which your program controls directly. If you really need to adjust hard limits, you'll need elevated permissions because the kernel protects those boundaries from regular users for security reasons.
Okay so I should just stick with soft limits in my Rust code and avoid hard limits unless absolutely necessary?

Exactly. Stick with soft limits in your Rust code using the set_rlimit trait to configure memory or file descriptor caps safely. This approach keeps your application flexible while respecting system constraints without needing special permissions.

I've read about the nix rlimit and setting ulimits programmatically in the docs, but it still feels like magic to me. I can't seem to grasp how Rust actually enforces memory or file descriptor caps at the system level.

That's a great question because understanding why this exists is key. In Linux, every process has hard and soft limits on resources like memory and open files, managed by the kernel via setrlimit syscalls. Rust doesn't just wrap these; it exposes them through traits that let you configure resource constraints before your program even runs. For instance, you can use the std::io::Cursor to simulate streams safely while the nix crate's rlimit module lets you call the actual system calls to enforce those caps.
Okay, so I want to limit memory usage specifically. How do I actually apply that in code?

You can use the nix crate's rlimit module to set ulimits programmatically before launching your process. The trait implementation for this allows you to specify a resource type like memory or file descriptors, then pass in the desired limit value. For example, you'd call a function on the rlimit object that wraps the underlying syscalls to change the soft and hard limits, ensuring your application never exceeds what you defined.
But what if the system rejects my request? Do I just catch errors?

You do, but there's more to it. When you attempt to modify limits via setrlimit syscalls, the kernel might return an error indicating that the requested limit exceeds your current hard limit or is invalid for security reasons. In Rust, this typically manifests as a failure on the low-level call which propagates up through the nix crate's abstractions. You need to handle these cases carefully because some limits are immutable once set at boot time.
Can I check what limits are currently active before trying to change them?

Yes, you can query existing values using the same rlimit module. The trait provides methods to retrieve both soft and hard limits for any given resource type like memory or file descriptors. This is useful when debugging why a setrlimit call failed earlier; by comparing current vs requested values, you'll see exactly where the mismatch occurred.
Alright, one more thing: what happens if I try to increase file descriptor limits too much?

If you attempt to raise the limit beyond your hard cap defined by system configuration, the setrl syscall will fail with an error. The nix crate's rlimit module handles this gracefully by returning a specific error code rather than panicking. You can then log this failure and optionally retry with adjusted values or inform the user that their application needs elevated privileges to modify such critical resources.
Thanks! Just one last check: is there a way to make these limits persistent across restarts?

Not directly through runtime code alone. Once you set ulimits programmatically via nix's rlimit module, they apply only for that process instance. To persist them across system reboots, you'd need to configure /etc/security/limits.conf on Linux or systemd unit files depending on your setup. The Rust layer handles the execution-time enforcement, but the underlying OS policies require external configuration changes.
